title: One Question Answering Model for Many Languages with Cross-lingual Dense Passage Retrieval
paper_text:
One Question Answering Model for Many Languages with Cross-lingual Dense Passage Retrieval



Anonymous Author(s) AfﬁliationAddress email



Abstract We present CORA , a C ross-lingual O pen- R etrieval A nswer Generation model 1 that can answer questions across many languages even when language-speciﬁc 2 annotated data or knowledge sources are unavailable. We introduce a new dense 3 passage retrieval algorithm that is trained to retrieve documents across languages 4 for a question. Combined with a multilingual autoregressive generation model, 5 CORA answers directly in the target language without any external translation or 6 in-language retrieval modules as used in prior work. To train CORA, we extend 7 available annotated data using an iterative training approach that improves retrieval 8 for languages with limited resources. Our results show that CORA substantially 9 outperforms the previous state of the art on multilingual open question answering 10 benchmarks across 26 languages, 9 of which are unseen during training. Our 11 analyses show the signiﬁcance of cross-lingual retrieval and generation in many 12 languages, particularly under low-resource settings. We will publicly release our 13 code and model. 14



1 Introduction 15

 Multilingual open QA is the task of answering a question from a large collection of multilingual 16 documents. Most recent progress in open QA is made for English by building a pipeline consisting 17 of a dense passage retriever trained on large-scale English QA datasets to ﬁnd evidence passages in 18 English (Lee et al., 2019; Karpukhin et al., 2020)

,

 followed by a reader to extracts the answer from 19 retrieved passages. However, extending this approach to multilingual open QA poses new challenges. 20 Answering many questions requires retrieving answers in knowledge sources of other languages than 21 the original question since many languages have limited reference documents or the question inquires 22 about concepts from other cultures (Asai et al., 2021; Lin et al., 2020). Yet, large-scale open QA 23 training data are not available across languages. 24 To address these challenges, previous work in multilingual open QA (Ture and Boschee, 2016; Asai 25 et al., 2021) translates questions into English, applies an English open QA system to answer in 26 English, and then translates answers back to the target language. This pipeline approach suffers 27 from error propagation of the machine translation component into the downstream QA, especially for 28 low-resource languages. Moreover, it is not able to answer questions whose answers can be found 29 in resources written in languages other than English or the target languages. For example, when a 30 Japanese question asks about a Korean actor, it is useful to retrieve passages from Korean Wikipedia, 31 not just English and Japanese. In this paper, we introduce a single QA model that answers questions 32 in any language leveraging an iterative training and data expansion approach to enable cross-lingual 33 retrieval and answer generation. 34 Our method (called CORA, Fig. 1) extends the retrieve-then-generate approach of English open QA 35 with a single cross-lingual retriever and a generator that do not rely on language-speciﬁc or machine 36 Submitted to 35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021). Do not distribute. The multilingual retrieval module produces dense embeddings of a question and 37 all multilingual passages, thereby retrieving passages across languages. The generation component is 38 trained to output an answer in the target language conditioned on the retrieved multilingual passages. 39 Those two components are iteratively trained to address limitations of the current multilingual QA 40 datasets. In particular, each iteration proceeds over two stages by updating model parameters with 41 available training data and expanding the training data cross-lingually by Wikipedia language links 42 and model predictions. This approach does not require any additional human annotations or machine 43 translation, and can be applied to many new languages with low resources. 44 Our experiments show that CORA advances the state of the art on two multilingual open QA datasets, 45 X OR -T Y D I QA (Asai et al., 2021) and MKQA (Longpre et al., 2020), across 26 typologically 46 diverse languages; CORA achieves gains of 21.4 and 4.9 F1 points in X OR -T Y D I QA and MKQA 47 respectively, where MKQA data is not used for training. Moreover, CORA achieves F1 scores of 48 roughly 30 over 9 languages on MKQA that have no training data or even reference Wikipedia 49 documents, outperforming the state-of-the-art approach by 5.4 F1. Our controlled experiments and 50 human analyses illustrate the impact of cross-lingual retrieval in improving QA performance. For 51 example, we observe that CORA can ﬁnd answers to 20% of the multilingual QA questions that are 52 valid but are originally labeled as unanswerable, by using cross-lingual retrieval. 53

2 Method

 54 We deﬁne multilingual open QA as the task of answering a question q L in a target language L given 55 a collection of multilingual reference passages C multi , where evidence passages can be retrieved 56 from any language . This deﬁnition extends the open QA setting proposed by Asai et al. (2021) as 57 they assume the reference documents are either in English or the target language. These passages can 58 come from Wikipedia articles that are not necessarily parallel over languages. We introduce CORA, 59 which runs a retrieve-then-generate procedure to achieve this goal (Fig. 1). 60

2.1 CORA Inference

 61 CORA directly retrieves multilingual passages for questions in any language and generates answers 62 in the target language conditioned on those retrieved passages. More formally, CORA inference 63 consists of two steps of (i) retrieving passages p multi and (ii) generating the answer a L from the 64 retrieved passages. 65 , Multilingual Dense Passage Retriever (mDPR). mDPR extends Dense Passage Retriever (DPR; 66 Karpukhin et al., 2020) to a multilingual setting. mDPR uses an iterative training approach to 67 ﬁne-tune a pre-trained multilingual language model (e.g., mBERT; Devlin et al., 2019) to encode 68 passages and questions separately (§ 2.2). Once training is done, the representations for all passages 69 from C multi are computed ofﬂine and stored in local storage. At inference, mDPR independently 70 obtains d -dimensional ( d = 768 ) encodings of the question e q L = mBERT q ( q L ) and a passage 71 e p L = mBERT p ( p ) , where a passage p is a ﬁxed-length sequence of tokens from multilingual 72 documents. It retrieves k passages with the k highest relevance scores to the question, where the 73 2 Initial QA data mDPR 1 mGEN 1 mGEN 2 mDPR 2 Negative Training Inference mDPR retrieved data WikiData Positive New training data 1. Initial mDPR Training 2. Training mGEN from mDPR data 3. Cross-lingual Data Expansion 4. Automatic Labeling Wikidata-based data 5. Iteratively training mDPR & mGEN mGEN 1 outputs: ✔ ❌ relevance score between a passage p and a question q L is estimated by the inner product of the 74 encoding vectors, ⟨ e q L , e p ⟩ . The retrieved passages p multi can be in any language in C multi . 75 Multilingual Answer Generator (mGEN). We use a multilingual sequence-to-sequence model 76 (e.g., mT5; Xue et al., 2021) to generate answers in the target language token-by-token autoregres- 77 sively given the retrieved multilingual passages p multi . We choose a generation approach because it 78 can generate an answer in the target language L from passages across different languages. 1 Moreover, 79 the generator can be adapted to new and unseen languages, some of which may have little or no 80 translation training data. Speciﬁcally, the generator outputs the sequence probability for a L as follows: 81 where a Li denotes the i -th token in the answer, and T is the length of the answer. We append a 83 language tag to the question to indicate the target language. We can remove the dependency on 84 p multi and skip the retrieval step (i.e., close-book model), but this results in a performance drop (§ 4). 85

2.2 CORA Training

 86 We introduce an iterative training approach that encourages cross-lingual retrieval and answer 87 generation conditioned on multilingual passages (sketched in Fig. 2 and Alg. 1). Each iteration 88 proceeds over two stages: parameter updates (§ 2.2.1) where mDPR and mGEN are trained 89 given the current training data and cross-lingual data expansion (§ 2.2.2) where training data are 90 automatically expanded by Wikipedia language links and model predictions. Our data expansion 91 addresses the limitation of the current multilingual QA datasets. 92 Initial training data. Training data is initialized by combining multilingual QA (T Y D I QA, Clark 93 et al., 2020 and X OR -T Y D I QA) and English open QA datasets (Natural Questions, Kwiatkowski 94 et al., 2019). One training instance from these datasets comprises a question, a positive passage, and 95 an answer. For each question, we use positive passages for the other questions in the training batch as 96 negative passages (Karpukhin et al., 2020). Note that annotations in the multilingual QA datasets 97 are limited: positive passages are taken either from English (Asai et al., 2021) or the question’s 98 language (Clark et al., 2020). Further, most of the non-English languages are not covered. Indeed, 99 when we only train mDPR on this initial set, it learns to retrieve passages in the same languages or 100 similar languages with irrelevant context. 101

2.2.1 Parameter Updates

After updating mDPR, we use mDPR to retrieve top k passages 107 p multi for each q L . Given these pairs of the question and the retrieved passages ( q L , p multi ) as input, 108 mGEN is trained to generate answer a L autoregressively (Eq. (1)) and minimize the cross-entropy 109 loss. To train the model to generate in languages not covered by the original datasets, we translate a L 110 to other languages using Wikipedia language links and create new synthetic mGEN training data. 2 111

2.2.2 Cross-lingual Data Expansion

 112 After the parameter updates, we mine new training data using mDPR and Wikipedia language links 113 and label the new data by mGEN predictions. This step is skipped in the ﬁnal iteration. 114 Expansion by trained mDPR and language links (line 6 in Alg. 1). Trained mDPR can discover 115 positive passages in another language that are not covered by initial training data. At each iteration, 116 we use retrieved passages p multi for q L (lines 4 in Alg. 1) as a source of new positive and negative 117 passages. This enables expanding data between language pairs not in the original data. To add more 118 languages, we use language links between Wikipedia articles and Wikidata entities. 119 Wikipedia maintains article-level language links that connect articles on the same entity over lan- 120 guages. We use these links to expand training data from the English QA dataset of Natural Questions. 121 Denote a training instance by ( q Eng , a Eng , p gold ) . We ﬁrst translate the English answer a Eng to a 122 target language a L by language links. We use language links again to look up the English Wikipedia 123 article that the gold passage p gold comes from. We then ﬁnd articles in non-English languages in the 124 reference documents that correspond to this article. We add passages from these articles to p multi as 125 positive passage candidates, which are passed to mGEN to evaluate if they lead to a L or not. 126 Automatic labeling by mGEN predictions (lines 7-8 in Alg. 1). A passage p i from p multi may not 127 always provide sufﬁcient information to answer the question q L . We take instances generated from 128 the two expansion methods described above, and run mGEN on each passage to predict an answer for 129 the question. If the answer matches the correct answer a L , then the passage p i is labeled as a positive 130 passage ; otherwise we label the input passage as a negative passage . We add these new positive and 131 negative passages to the training data, and in the next iteration, mDPR is trained on this expanded 132 training set (§ 2.2.1). 133

3 Experiments

 134 We evaluate CORA on two multilingual open QA datasets across 28 typologically diverse languages. 3 135 CORA achieves state-of-the-art performance across 26 languages, and greatly outperforms previous 136 approaches that use language-speciﬁc models such as question translation. 137 2 This data augmentation is only done after the third epoch of initial training to prevent the model from overﬁtting to synthetic data . See the appendix for more detail. 3 A full list of the language family and script types are in the appendix . 4

3.1 Datasets and Knowledge Sources

 138 Multilingual open QA datasets differ in covered languages, annotation schemes, and target application 139 scenarios. We evaluate F1 and EM scores over the questions with answer annotations from two 140 datasets, following the common evaluation practice in open QA (Lee et al., 2019). 141 X OR -T Y D I QA. X OR -T Y D I QA (Asai et al., 2021) is a multilingual open QA dataset consisting 142 of 7 languages, where questions are originally from T Y D I QA (Clark et al., 2020) and posed by 143 information-seeking native speakers. The answers are annotated by extracting spans from Wikipedia 144 in the same language of the question ( in-language data ) or by translating English spans extracted 145 from English Wikipedia to the target language ( cross-lingual data ). 146 MKQA. MKQA (Longpre et al., 2020) is an evaluation dataset created by translating 10k Natural 147 Questions (Kwiatkowski et al., 2019) to 25 target languages. The parallel data enables us to compare 148 the models’ performance across typologically diverse languages. This dataset does not have any 149 training data, and only ﬁve languages are covered in the X OR -T Y D I QA training data. 150 Collection of multilingual documents. We use the Wikipedia dumps of 13 diverse languages from 151 all X OR -T Y D I QA languages and a subset of MKQA languages, leading to about 31.5M passages. 4 152 Several MKQA languages are not included so that we can evaluate over unseen languages and make 153 indexing and inference computationally feasible. We chose the 13 languages to cover languages with 154 a large number of Wikipedia articles and a variety of both Latin and non-Latin scripts. 155 Training-evaluation language splits. CORA is trained on X OR -T Y D I QA; mDPR uses gold 156 passages from languages in X OR -T Y D I QA and also synthetically mined data from 13 languages (as 157 explained in § 2.2.2). The mGEN model is trained on X OR -T Y D I QA and synthetically generated 158 data for 17 languages (§ 2.2.1). We call languages on which we train CORA with annotated data or 159 synthetically generated data seen languages, while call others unseen languages. MKQA is only used 160 for evaluation, and 9 of its languages are never included during training. 161

3.2 Baselines and Experimental Setting

 162 We compare CORA with the following strong baselines adopted from Asai et al. (2021). 163 Translate-test (MT + DPR). As used in most previous work (e.g., Asai et al., 2021), this method 164 translates the question to English, extracts an answer in English using DPR, and then translates the 165 answer back to the target language. The translation models are obtained from MarianMT (Junczys- 166 Dowmunt et al., 2018) and trained on the OPUS-MT dataset (Tiedemann, 2012). 167 Monolingual baseline (BM 25). This baseline retrieves passages in the target language and extracts 168 the answer from the retrieved passages. Due to the lack of training data in most of the target languages, 169 we use a BM25-based lexical Pyserini implementation (Lin et al., 2021). We then feed the retrieved 170 documents to a multilingual QA model to extract ﬁnal answers. 171 MT+Mono. This baseline combines results from the translate-test method (MT+DPR) and the 172 monolingual method (BM25) to enable retrieving passages in both English and the target language. 173 Following Asai et al. (2021), we prioritize predictions from the monolingual pipeline if they are over 174 a certain threshold; otherwise we output predictions from the translate-test method. 5 175 Closed-book baseline. This model uses an mT5-base sequence-to-sequence model that takes a 176 question as input and generates answers in the target language without any retrieval component at 177 inference time (Roberts et al., 2020). This baseline assesses the models’ ability of memorizing and 178 retrieving knowledge from its parameters without using the knowledge from reference documents. 179 CORA details. For all experiments, we use a single retriever (mDPR) and a generator (mGEN) that 180 use the same passage embeddings. mDPR uses multilingual-BERT-base-uncased, 6 and the generator 181 uses mT5-base for ﬁne-tuning. We ﬁrst ﬁne-tune mDPR using gold passages from Natural Question, 182 and then further ﬁne-tune it using X OR -T Y D I QA and T Y D I QA’s gold passage data. We exclude 183 4 Downloaded from https://archive.org/details/wikimediadownloads?and%5B%5D= year%3A%222019%22 . 5 For the languages not supported by Pyserini, we always output translate-test’ predictions. 6 XLM-RoBERTa (Conneau et al., 2020) did not improve our results. 5 the training questions in Natural Questions and T Y D I QA used to create the MKQA or X OR -T Y D I 184 QA evaluation set. We run two iterations of CORA training. All hyperparameters are in the appendix. 185

4 Results and Analysis

4.1 QA Results

 187 X OR -T Y D I QA. Table 1 reports the scores of CORA and the baselines in X OR -T Y D I QA. CORA, 188 which only uses a single retriever and a single generator, outperforms the baselines and the previous 189 state-of-the-art model on X OR -T Y D I QA by a large margin across all 7 languages. CORA achieves 190 a gain of 21.4 macro-averaged F1 points over the previous state-of-the-art method, which uses the 191 black-box APIs of Google Translate and Google Custom Search. 192 MKQA. Tables 2 and 3 report the F1 scores of CORA and the baselines on MKQA under seen and 193 unseen settings. 7 Longpre et al. (2020) only report results in a simpliﬁed setting with gold passages 194 given in advance, where retrieval is unnecessary. CORA signiﬁcantly outperforms the baselines in all 195 languages by a large margin except for Arabic and English. CORA yields larger improvements from 196 the translate-test baseline in the languages that are distant from English and with limited training 197 data such as Malay (MS; 29.2 vs. 12.6) and Hebrew (He; 15.3 vs. 8.9). The performance drop 198 of the translate-test model from English to other languages indicates the error propagation from 199 the translation process. BM25 performs very poorly in some low-resource languages such as Thai 200 because of the lack of answer content in the target languages’ Wikipedia. MT+Mono underpeforms 201 the MT+DPR baseline in MKQA since it is challenging to rerank answers from two separate methods 202 with uncaliberated conﬁdence scores. In contrast, CORA uses a single retrieval model to retrieve 203 passages across languages, achieving around 30 F1 in a majority of the 26 languages. 204 Ablations: Impact of CORA components. We compare CORA with the following three variants 205 to study the impact of different components on X OR -T Y D I QA and a subset of MKQA ( ∼ 5% of 206 the evaluation set). (i) mDPR 1 + mGEN 1 only trains CORA using labeled annotated data and 207 7 Following previous work in open QA, but different from the ofﬁcial script of MKQA (Longpre et al., 2020), we disregard the questions labeled as “no answer” to ensure that a model cannot increase aggregate F1 and EM scores simply by predicting “no answer.” As shown in our human analysis, it is difﬁcult to prove an answer does not exist in the millions of multilingual documents even if the annotation says so. 6 measures the impact of the iterative training. (ii) DPR (trained NQ) + mGEN replaces mDPR 208 with a multilingual BERT-based DPR trained on English data from Natural Questions (NQ) and 209 encodes all passages in C multi . This conﬁguration assesses the impact of cross-lingual training data. 210 (iii) CORA, C multi ={Eng.} only retrieves from English during inference. This variant evaluates if 211 English reference documents sufﬁce to answer multilingual questions. 212 Results in Table 4 show performance drops in all three variants. This leads to the following conclu- 213 sions: (i) iterative learning and data mining process is useful, (ii) mDPR trained with cross-lingual 214 data outperforms DPR with multi-lingual BERT, and (iii) languages other than English are important 215 in answering multilingual questions. 216

Table 6 analyzes errors made by CORA by manually 243 inspecting 50 Japanese and Spanish wrong predictions from MKQA. We observe six error categories: 244 (a) retrieval error, (b) generation language error (different lang), (c) incorrect answer generation 245 (incorrect answer), (d) answer annotation errors (e.g., a correct alias isn’t covered by gold answers, or 246 Wikipedia information is inconsistent with English.), and (e) ambiguous or ill-formed questions such 247 as “who won X this year” (invalid question). The table shows that in both Japanese and Spanish, the 248 retrieval errors are dominant. 5 Related Work and Broader Impacts 269

 English and non-English open QA. Despite the rapid progress in open QA (Chen et al., 2017; 270 Karpukhin et al., 2020; Lewis et al., 2020; Izacard and Grave, 2021), most prior work has been 271 exclusively on English. Several prior attempts to build multilingual open QA often rely on additional 272 MT or language-speciﬁc retrieval models (Asai et al., 2021; Ture and Boschee, 2016). 273 Many-languages-one models. Recently, several work introduced single multilingual models for 274 many languages by using pre-trained multilingual models such as mBERT or mT5 in several NLP 275 tasks (e.g., entity linking: Botha et al., 2020; De Cao et al., 2021; semantic role labeling: Mulcaire 276 et al., 2019; Lyu et al., 2019; Fei et al., 2020). This work conducts the ﬁrst large-scale study 277 of a uniﬁed multilingual open QA model across many languages, and we show state-of-the-art 278 performance in 26 typologically diverse languages. 279 Synthetic data creation for multilingual machine reading comprehension. Several recent work 280 augment multilingual machine reading comprehension training data by generating new question 281 and answer pairs from randomly sampled non-English Wikipedia paragraphs using a sequence-to- 282 sequence model (Riabi et al., 2020; Shakeri et al., 2020). This work focuses on multilingual open QA, 283 which involve cross-lingual retrieval going beyond extracting answers from multilingual passages. 284 To this end, we augment existing training data by using trained models and language links, which can 285 be further improved by applying similar question generation methods. 286 Societal impacts. We will make our code publicly available. CORA can perform open QA in unseen 287 languages and could beneﬁt the society in building QA systems for low-resource languages such as 288 African languages, hence enabling research in that direction. By removing the necessity of using 289 external blackbox APIs, we can examine and address wrong answers because of its prediction error 290 or misinformation that exists in Wikipedia. This would help us to mitigate the potential negative 291 impact by CORA outputting a wrong answer when it used by people who seek information. 292

6 Conclusion

 293 To address the information needs from many non-English speakers, a QA system has to conduct 294 cross-lingual passage retrieval. This work presents CORA, a uniﬁed multilingual open QA model 295 that retrieves multilingual passages and generates answers in the target languages, conditioned on 296 the retrieved passages. CORA does not require language-speciﬁc translation or retrieval components 297 and can even answer questions in unseen new languages. We conduct extensive experiments on two 298 multilingual open QA datasets across 28 languages, 26 of which CORA advances the state of the 299 art, outperforming competitive models by up to 20 F1 points. Our extensive analysis and manual 300 evaluation reveal that CORA effectively retrieves semantically relevant passages beyond language 301 boundaries, and can even ﬁnd answers to the questions that are previously considered unanswerable 302 due to lack of sufﬁcient evidence in annotation languages. 303 9

review.segment_text: Summary:

The paper attempts to provide a solution to an interesting problem of cross language retrieval based question answering in a zero shot (without requirement of any language-specific annotated training data).
Authors propose a new DPR algorithm that to be able to retrieve documents relevant to the question across languages (for multiple languages).
The paper extends retrieve-the-generate based open domain QA system to a cross-lingual setting.
Main Review:

How does the proposed approach differ in terms of techniques with something already proposed like RAG (https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401). It looks like the proposed approach is just a multilingual version of RAG thus the paper has limited novelty.
review.explanation: This work is not a simple extension of RAG. This work uses an iterative data expansion and training approach to train a model that is capable of cross-lingual dense passage retrieval.

review.segment_text: Does the system expects the gold document annotations to be available for training the retriever module?

Section 2.2., talks about positive passages, this requires huge time and effort for annotation, so how does authors claim in abstract that the proposed technique does not require any language specific annotated training data?
review.explanation: The proposed system can automatically mine new training data without new human annotation or using external modules such as machine translations.

review.segment_text: What happens in the scenario where a wikipedia page about a specific entity is not available in a particular language in which the question has been asked?
Section 2.2 needs to have some more intuition and clarity.
What is intuition behind translating English answers to target language.
Is there a mechanism to filter out spurious passages by the automatic passage labeling technique?
How does the model learns cross lingual embeddings?
do authors use a pre-trained model like m-bert?
How does the proposed model results compare when compared with m-bert based methods?
Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitations of the work has not been discussed by the authors.
Authors did promise to make the code publicly available to general public to enable further research in the direction ( societal impact)

review.segment_text: Summary:
This paper addresses the multilingual open QA task by combining a cross-lingual passage retrieval algorithm with a multilingual autoregressive generation model.
The approach is thus capable of answering questions where the answer and the question are in different languages.
The authors extended existing annotated data using an iterative training approach that improves retrieval for languages with limited resources.
The experimental results showed that the proposed method (CORA) outperformed the previous state of the art on multilingual open question answering benchmarks across 26 languages.
Main Review:
This paper targets at Multilingual Open Domain QA, which is a novel task with many application scenarios.
review.explanation: Multilingual Open Domain QA is not a new task

review.segment_text: The proposed method, CORA, showed relatively good performance on 26 languages on benchmark datasets.
However, the overall idea is very similar to CRISS [1].
The CRISS approach consists of a cross-lingual retrieval module and a seq2seq module, and they are optimized alternately.
Similarly, the CORA approach in this paper also consists of DPR (Dense Passage Retriever, mbert structure) and GEN(Answer Generator, mt5 structure), and they are also optimized alternately.
Although CRISS [1] doesn’t target at multilingual open QA, it has strong performance on cross-lingual sentence retrieval, which is related to multilingual open QA.
The authors should compare their work with CRISS [1].
Authors claim that they introduce a new dense passage retrieval algorithm, but don’t mention any similar work about dense passage retrieval [2].
review.explanation: The paper does cite some related work

review.segment_text: Overall, this paper combines DPR and GEN module and achieves good experimental results.
However, the technical contribution of the paper is quite limited.
[1] Cross-lingual Retrieval for Iterative Self-Supervised Training
[2] Approximate Nearest Neighbor Negative Contrastive Learning for Dense Text Retrieval
Limitations And Societal Impact:
I don't see potential negative social impact of this work.

review.segment_text: Summary:
This paper studies the task of multilingual Open-Domain Question Answering.
The task setting requires building a system that can answer natural language questions in any language on an open set of domains.
Models are required to answer in the same language the question was asked in.

The authors tackle the task using a fairly conventional retrieve-and-read ODQA model, with the necessary modifications to make both the retriever and reader component language-agnostic, by exploiting multi-lingual pretrained models, training on available multilingual training data, and using and a clever data augmentation setup to increase multilingual training data.
The proposed solution “CORA”, operates over wikipedias in many different languages.
The retrieval component “MDPR” is a multi-lingual extension of DPR, using MBERT.
This model can retrieve evidence documents across different languages, rather than having to match the language of the question.
This means that a question expressed in a low-resource language can benefit from retrieving evidence from high resource languages.
It also allows for a question written in one language on a cultural topic associated with a different language to retrieve from the most appropriate language’s wikipedia.

The reader “mGEN” is a generative reader, implemented using mT5, that takes as input the concatenation of the top-K retrieved passages from the retriever (which may be several different languages) and the question, and generates the answer in the question language, token by token.
The main difficulty of the proposed solution is how to train the MDPR and MGEN model components, given the limited multilingual raining data.
The initial model is trained with NQ (english) and TyDi QA and Xor-TiDi QA (multilingual data).
To increase the amount of multilingual training data and language coverage, the authors introduce a data augmentation strategy where the initial model is used to harvest potential additional data.
This exploits the wikipedia “language links” resource.

This iterative approach works by retrieving passages, looking up passages on the same topic in other languages using “language links”, and using the reader to see whether these can be used to generate the correct answer - if they can, they are added as additional training data for the next round.
Another expansion mechanism exploits the fact that most answers are wikipedia entities, and can be translated using annotations available in the wikipedia “language links” resource.
CORA is thoroughly evaluated on TyDI QA and an evaluation-only dataset called MKQA, and performs very strongly, improving the state-of-the-art by a wide margin in almost every setting..
Thorough ablations give insights and quantify the the effects of the system, and demonstrate zero-shot question answering abilities
Main Review:

This is an empirically very strong piece of work that performs sensible (if straightforward and somewhat incremental on the modelling side) extensions of work from open-domain QA and multilingual open-domain QA.
However, the result is stronger than the sum of its parts, unlocking the ability to combine information across many languages’ wikipedias, and enjoy the resultant improvements in performance.
This is a really good piece of work, and well executed.

The iterative training and data expansion techniques are interesting and important contributions, which leverage the wealth of annotations and metadata available in wikipedia to improve cross-lingual QA, without requiring explicit question translation or extra annotation efforts by humans.

One limitation is that this won’t always work well if the language links aren’t high quality for some languages, or in other domains where language links aren’t available (either because wikipedia isn’t a good background corpus, or because answers are not wikipedia/wikidata entities).
The experiments and ablations are thorough and detailed and the analysis is deep.

In terms of some constructive criticism, the MGen model could probably be made more powerful by using the Fusion-in-Decoder architecture, which would allow the model to scale to reading more passages per question, which has shown to greatly improve accuracy on open-domain QA.

I also wonder why the models’ performances are so different and sometimes inconsistent between MKQA and XOR-full (comparing tables 1 and 2) - for example, CORA scores 54.8 F1 for arabic on XOR-full, but loses a lot of performance arabic on MKQA (only 13.1 F1) .
Conversely, MT+DPR performs poorly for arabic on XOR-full (7.6 F1) but outperforms CORA on MKQA (16.0 F1).
Summary:
Originality: This paper takes a standard ODQA approach, but converts it to a massively cross-lingual setting in a novel and very powerful way.
( Quality: the experiments are well-thought out, the results are very strong, and the ablations and analysis are execllent
Clarity: The paper is very clear and well written
Significance: I expect this to be a high impact paper and serve as inspiration for many followup works and tasks.
Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors provide a note on societal impacts which summarize the main impacts well.
This kind of work will likely have a positive impact on societal equality, unlocking the strong ODQA performance we see in high resource languages for lower resource languages.
There are risks that CORA may provide wrong answers that might be harmful or offensive, but this is the case for monolingual systems too.
The authors rightly point out that not relying on external blackbox translators allows the model to be more interpretable and debuggable.
review.explanation: The neural network is still hard to interpret

review.segment_text: The use of wikipedia language links is useful, but does limit the textual domains the data expansion technique can be used for to similar domains to wikipedia.

review.segment_text: Summary:
This paper presents a multilingual dense passage retrieval model called mDPR to deal with the multilingual/cross-lingual IR and QA tasks.
The mDPR part is firstly trained using existing labeled multilingual Q-P pairs.
Then, possible passage pairs are selected from a given multilingual corpus based on the trained mDPR and used to train an answer generation model called mGEN.

Next, given each question, the trained mDPR is further used to retrieve more possible passage candidates in other languages from Wikipedia corpus, where the retrieved passages that can lead to correct answers are considered as positive instances, the others as negative instances.
With these expanded new question-passage pairs, the training of mDPR is performed again.
The above procedure is conducted in an iterative manner.
Evaluations are conducted on TyDi QA and MKQA in the open-domain QA setting.
Several baselines are included and compared.
The key contribution is to do DPR in the multilingual/cross-lingual setting.
review.explanation: This is only part of the contribution.

review.segment_text: Main Review:
This is a good paper that extends dense IR from monolingual to multilingual/cross-lingual.
review.explanation: This is not the key contribution of this paper

review.segment_text: But the novelty is limited.
The most important part, I think, is the data expansion part described in Section 2.2.
From model perspective, there seems be have few innovation.

The baselines compared in Table 1 and 2 are also weak. Why not compare with the submissions on the TyDi QA leaderboards? Why stronger multilingual pre-trained models such as XLM-R or mBART are not used and fine-tuned for comparison?
review.explanation: The baselines are not weak. TyDi QA leaderboard aims at machine reading comprehension where evidence paragraphs in the target language are given a priori and the task is to answer a question based on that paragraph.It does not require any retrieval and all of the questions can be answered based on the paragraphs written in the same language as the question. Those models in the leaderboard are not applicable .

review.segment_text: Limitations And Societal Impact:
no potential negative societal impact.

title: UniDoc: Unified Pretraining Framework for Document Understanding
paper_text:
UniDoc: Uniﬁed Pretraining Framework for Document Understanding

 Anonymous Author(s) AfﬁliationAddress email

Abstract Document intelligence automates the extraction of information from documents 1 and supports many business applications. Recent self-supervised learning methods 2 on large-scale unlabeled document datasets have opened up promising directions 3 towards reducing annotation efforts by training models with self-supervised ob- 4 jectives. However, most of the existing document pretraining methods are still 5 language-dominated. We present UniDoc, a new uniﬁed pretraining framework for 6 document understanding. UniDoc is designed to support most document under- 7 standing tasks, extending the Transformer to take multimodal embeddings as input. 8 Each input element is composed of words and visual features from a semantic re- 9 gion of the input document image. An important feature of UniDoc is that it learns 10 a generic representation by making use of three self-supervised losses, encourag- 11 ing the representation to model sentences, learn similarities, and align modalities. 12 Extensive empirical analysis demonstrates that the pretraining procedure learns 13 better joint representations and leads to improvements in downstream tasks.

 14

1 Introduction 15

 Document intelligence is a broad research area that includes techniques for information extraction and 16 understanding. Unlike plain-text documents in natural language processing (NLP) [1, 2, 3], a physical 17 document can be composed of multiple elements: tables, ﬁgures, charts

,

 etc . In addition, a document 18 usually includes rich visual information

,

 and can be one of various types of documents (scientiﬁc 19 paper, form, resume

,

 etc .), with various combinations of multiple elements and layouts. Complex 20 content and layout, noisy data, font and style variations make automatic document understanding very 21 challenging. For example, to understand text-rich documents such as letters, a system needs to focus 22 almost exclusively on text content, paying attention to a long sequential context, while processing 23 semi-structured documents such as forms requires the system to analyze spatially distributed short 24 words, paying particular attention to the spatial arrangement of the words. Following the success 25 of BERT [4] on NLP tasks, there has been growing interest in developing pretraining methods 26 for document understanding [5, 6, 7, 8]. Pretrained models have achieved state-of-the-art (SoTA) 27 performance across diverse document understanding tasks [9, 10]. 28 Huge training datasets help pretraining models to learn a good representation for downstream tasks, 29 however, we observe three major problems with the current pretraining setup: (1) documents are 30 composed of semantic regions . Most of the recent document pretraining works follow BERT and split 31 documents into words. However, unlike the sequence-to-sequence learning in NLP, documents have a 32 hierarchical structure (words form sentences, sentences form a semantic region, and semantic regions 33 form a document). Also, the importance of words and sentences are highly context-dependent, i.e ., 34 the same word or sentence may have different importance in a different context. Moreover, current 35 transformer-based document pretraining models suffer from input length constraints. Also, input 36 Submitted to 35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021). Do not distribute. length becomes a problem for text-rich documents or multi-page documents. (2) documents are 37 more than words . The semantic structure of the document is not only determined by the text within 38 it but also the visual features such as table, font size and style, and ﬁgure, etc . Moreover, the 39 visual appearance of the text within a block are often overlooked. Most of recent BERT-based pre- 40 training works only take the words as input without considering multimodal content and alignment 41 of multimodal information within semantic regions. (3) documents have spatial layout . Visual and 42 layout information is critical for document understanding. Recent works encode spatial information 43 via 2D position encoding and model spatial relationships with self-attention, which computes attention 44 weights for long inputs [5, 6]. However, for semi-structured documents, such as forms and receipts, 45 words are more related to their local surroundings. This corresponds strongly with human intuition – 46 when we look at magazines or newspapers, the receptive ﬁelds are modulated by our reading order 47 and attention. Based on the above observations, we ask the following question: can uniﬁed document 48 pretraining beneﬁt all of these different kinds of documents? 49 We propose a uniﬁed pretraining framework for document understanding, shown in Fig. 1. Our 50 model integrates image information in the pretraining stage by taking advantage of the transformer 51 architecture to learn cross-modal interactions between visual and textual information. To handle 52 textual information, we encode sentences using a hierarchical transformer encoder. The ﬁrst level 53 of the hierarchical encoder models the formation of the sentences from words. The second level 54 models the formation of the document from sentences. With the help of the hierarchical structure, 55 UniDoc learns how words form sentences and how sentences form documents. Meanwhile, it reduces 56 model computation complexity exponentially and increases the number of input words. This also 57 mimics human reading behaviors since the sentence/paragraph is a reasonable unit for people to 58 read and understand—people rarely check the interactions between arbitrary words across different 59 regions in order to understand an article. Convolution has been very successful in the extraction of 60 local features that encode visual and spatial information [11], so we use convolution layers as a more 61 efﬁcient complement to self-attention for addressing local intra-region dependencies in a document 62 image. Meanwhile, self-attention uses all input tokens to generate attention weights for capturing 63 global dependencies. Thus, we combine convolution with self-attention to form a mixed attention 64 mechanism that combines the advantages of the two operations. 65 We depart from previous vision-language pretraining [12, 13] by extracting both the textual and 66 visual features for each semantic region. We propose a novel gated cross-attentional transformer 67 that enables information exchange between modalities. A visually-rich region (ﬁgure, chart, etc ) 68 may have stronger visual information than textual information. Instead of treating outputs from both 69 modalities identically, we design a gating mechanism that can dynamically control the inﬂuence of 70 textual and visual features. This approach enables cross-modal connections and allows for variable 71 highlight the relevant information in visual and textual modality and enables cross-modal connections. 72 During pretraining, the CNN-based visual backbone and multi-layer gated cross-attention encoder 73 are jointly trained in both pretraining and ﬁne-tuning phase. 74 Our contributions are summarized as follows: (1) We introduce UniDoc, a powerful pretraining 75 framework for document understanding. UniDoc is capable of learning contextual textual and 76 visual information and cross-modal correlations within a single framework, which leads to better 77 performance. (2) We present Masked Sentence Modeling for language modeling, Visual Contrastive 78 Learning for vision modeling, and Vision-Language Alignment for pretraining. (3) We present 79 extensive experiments and analyses to validate the effectiveness of the proposed UniDoc. Extensive 80 experiments and analysis provide useful insights on the effectiveness of the pretraining tasks and 81 show outstanding performance on various downstream tasks. 82

2 Related Work

 83 Self-supervised learning has shown great success in producing generic representations that learn 84 from large-scale unlabeled corpora [4]. Like the development of pretraining in computer vision [14] 85 and NLP [4], there has been a surging interest in self-supervised learning for Vision-Language 86 (VL) tasks [12, 15, 13, 16, 17, 18]. Transformers [4] are the key technology that enables learning 87 contextualized representations from large-scale unlabeled training data. These methods generally 88 employ BERT-like objectives to learn cross-modal representations from visual region features and 89 word embeddings. A major difference between natural images and document images is that documents 90 2 . trained during are designed by humans. Unlike the image-caption pairs used in VL, document images do not have 91 captions, and the visual and textual information are co-occur within semantic regions. 92 Several recent works have explored pretraining on document images [5, 6, 19, 20]. LayoutLM [5] 93 extends BERT to learn contextualized word representations for document images through multi-task 94 learning. It takes a sequence of OCR words as input during pretraining and incorporates the 2D 95 position embedding as input for each token. However, LayoutLM only considers textual information 96 during pretraining without modeling the alignment between visual and textual information–visual 97 information is only incorporated into the model during the ﬁne-tuning stage. The most recent 98 version, LayoutLMv2 [6], improves on this by incorporating the image encoder into pretraining and 99 jointly training the image encoder along with the BERT model. LayoutLMv2 splits the document 100 image into several parts and concatenates the visual embeddings and text embeddings into a single 101 sequence. Apart from masked token learning for language, LayoutLMv2 also considers image-text 102 alignment and image-text matching during pretraining. LAMPERT [8] proposes a structure that 103 exploits pretraining tasks to learn a hierarchical framework. It uses the English Wikipedia pages as the 104 target dataset, which provides the parsed content blocks. The most related work to ours is SelfDoc [7], 105 which proposes a multimodal document pretraining framework. It ﬁrst extracts the document object 106 proposals from pre-trained Faster R-CNN [21] and then applies OCR for each proposal to get the 107 words. It takes the pre-extracted RoI features and sentence embeddings as input, and models the 108 perform learning over the textual and visual information using the cross-modality encoder. 109 There is a noticeable difference between our proposed method, UniDoc, and other concurrent works 110 in document pretraining. Unlike the ﬁxed document object detector in [7], the parameters of the 111 image encoder with RoI align, which derive the visual features for semantic regions, are also updated 112 in UniDoc. In contrast to [6], our visual features come from the semantic regions instead of splitting 113 the image into ﬁxed regions. Moreover, to learn the contextualized visual representations, UniDoc 114 masks visual information in the latent space and learns contextualized representations by solving a 115 contrastive learning task deﬁned over a quantization of the latent visual embeddings. 116

3 Method

 117

3.1 Model Architecture

 118 Fig. 1 illustrates our approach, UniDoc, which consists of four components: feature extraction, feature 119 embedding, multi-layer gated cross-attention encoder, and pretraining tasks. Given a document image 120 and the locations of document elements (sentence or RoI), UniDoc takes image regions and words 121 that correspond to each document elements as inputs, and extracts their respective embeddings 122 through a visual feature extractor and a sentence encoder. These embeddings are then fed into a 123 transformer-based encoder to learn the cross-modal contextualized embeddings that integrate both 124 visual features and textual features. 125 3 In the feature extraction step, we ﬁrst employ an off-the-shelf OCR tool [22] to extract text from a doc- 126 ument image I , where the words are grouped into sentences S = { s 1 , . . . , s N } whose corresponding 127 bounding boxes are P = { p 1 , . . . , p N } . For each sentence bounding box p i , we use a ConvNet-based 128 backbone f ImEnc and RoI Align [23] f RoIAlign to extract the pooled RoI features v i . To obtain a feature 129 embedding , we extract the sentence embedding s i for each sentence s i via a pretrained sentence 130 encoder f SentEnc . Each region’s RoI feature v i is discretized into a ﬁnite set of visual representations 131 v Qi ∈ V Q via product quantization [24]. The multi-layer Gated Cross-Attention encoder takes the 132 position information, masked visual features ˜V and masked textual features ˜S as inputs, and then it 133 generates the contextualized multimodal representations ( H lV and H lS , l ∈ [1 , L ] ) and outputs the 134 predicted features ( ˆV and ˆS ), where L is the number of stacked transformer blocks. 135 More formally, the pretraining procedure can be decomposed into the following steps: 136 where f Mask denotes the masking function that randomly masks RoI features and sentence embeddings 137 with the respective probabilities p v Mask and p s Mask . L Pretraining is composed of three pretraining tasks: 138 Masked Sentence Modeling (MSM), Visual Contrastive Learning (VCL), and Vision-Language 139 Alignment (VLA). Next, we provide details mentioned in Eq. 1. 140 Feature Extraction and Embedding. Formally, a document image I ∈ R W × H consists 141 of N regions, where each region’s bounding box is characterized by a 6-d vector, as p i = 142 { x LT W , y LT H , x RB W , y RB H , wW , hH } , where w and h are of the width and height the region, W and H are the 143 width and height of I , while ( x LT , y LT ) and ( x RB , y RB ) denote the coordinates of the top-left and 144 bottom-right corners respectively. The 6-d vector is mapped into a high-dimensional representation 145 via a linear mapping function. 146 The visual embedding is the sum of the mapped RoI feature and position embedding. Likewise, the 147 textual embedding is the sum of sentence embedding and position embedding. We also have different 148 types of segments to distinguish different modalities. The input sequence to the transformer-based 149 encoder starts with a special start element ([CLS] and full visual features), then it is followed by 150 multimodal elements, and it ends with a special ending element ([SEP]+full visual features). For 151 the special elements ([CLS] and [SEP]), the corresponding full visual features are features extracted 152 from the whole input image, by applying f ImEnc to an RoI covering the whole input image. 153 Quantization Module. Unlike the ﬁxed image encoder in [7], we jointly learn the image encoder 154 in an end-to-end fashion alongside the multimodal model. A visual representation can be learned 155 by predicting the visual features of the masked regions, but it is challenging to predict such features 156 exactly, since they are unconstrained and of continuous representation. To constrain the representation 157 space of the visual features and facilitate the end-to-end learning of image encoder (see Task #2 158 in Sec. 3.2) , we follow [25, 26] and use vector quantization to discretize the visual features V = 159 { v 1 , . . . , v N } into a ﬁnite set of representations V Q = { v Q 1 , . . . , v QN } . Speciﬁcally, we deﬁne latent 160 embedding spaces e ∈ R C × E , where C is the number of codebooks, and E is the number of entries 161 for each codebook. For each v i , we ﬁrst map it to logits v (cid:96)i ∈ R C × E , and calculate the probability 162 for the j -th codebook entry in i -th group as p c,e = exp (( v (cid:96)c,e + g e ) /τ ) / (cid:80) Ek =1 exp (( v (cid:96)c,k + g k ) /τ ) , 163 where τ is a non-negative temperature, g 1: E are i.i.d samples drawn from Gumbel(0,1) distribution. 164 During the forward pass, we choose one entry vector from each codebook by ˜ e i ∼ argmax e p c,e 165 and generate the quantized representation v Qi by a concatenation of { ˜ e 1 , . . . , ˜ e G } which is then 166 followed by a linear transformation. During the backward pass, the gradients are computed through a 167 Gumbel-Softmax estimator [27]. 168 Gated Cross-Attention. To model the interactions among multimodal inputs, we introduce a 169 multimodal transformer with gated cross-attention to model the cross-modality relationships. Let 170 H l +1 m be output features at the l -th layer for one modality m , and let n be another modality ( m, n ∈ 171 { V, S } ). We obtain the features at ( l + 1) -th layer as: 172 where f LN denotes layer normalization [28]. The feed-forward sub- layer f FF in Eq. 2 is further 173 composed of two fully-connected sub-layers, both wrapped in residual adds and f LN . 174 4 The core part of Eq. 2 is the cross-attention f Cross-Att ( · ) . Given the intermediate representations H lm 175 and H ln , the cross-attention output for modality m is computed as: 176 where f iq ( H lm ) , f ik ( H ln ) , and f iv ( H ln ) are the query , key , and value calculated by linear mapping 177 layers for the i -th head. d is the model dimension, h is the number of heads, and U is the weight 178 matrix that combines the outputs of the heads. 179 Considering the substantial diversity of document images and the different information needs of 180 differing document types, we use a gating mechanism [29] to dynamically weight the outputs of the 181 visual and textual branches. Speciﬁcally, we feed the concatenated the visual and textual features to a 182 non-linear network f Gate ([ H l +1 m ; H l +1 n ]) , which generates the modality-speciﬁc attention weights α lm 183 and α ln , and returns the weights separately to their respective modality-speciﬁc branches to perform 184 element-wise products. We multiply the features for modality m with its modality-speciﬁc attention 185 weight, and compute the updated feature as: H l +1 m = H l +1 m (1 + α lm ) , same that for modality n . 186

3.2 Training Tasks and Objectives

 187 The full pretraining objective of UniDoc (right block in Fig. 1) is deﬁned as: L Pretraining = L MSM + 188 L VCL + L VLA . In the rest of this section, we describe each task in detail. 189 Task #1 : Masked Sentence Modeling. This task is similar to the Masked Token Modeling task 190 utilized in BERT. The key difference is that we mask sentences instead of tokens. During pretraining, 191 each sentence and RoI of the input document is randomly and independently masked. For the masked 192 sentence, its token is replaced with a special sentence of [MASK]. The model is trained to predict 193 the masked sentence feature, based on the unmasked words and the visual features. The goal is to 194 predict the masked sentence embeddings based on the contextual information from the surrounding 195 sentences and image regions, by minimizing the smooth L1 loss [21]: 196 where Θ is the trainable parameters and f UniDoc ( . ) outputs the unmasked textual feature, s \ i is the 197 surrounding features for the i -th input, ˜V are the image features with random masking. 198 Task #2 : Visual Contrastive Learning. We learn visual feature representations by solving a visual 199 contrastive learning task which requires estimating the true quantized latent RoI representation. Given 200 a prediction ˆ v i ∈ ˆ V for the masked RoI ˜ v i ∈ ˜V , the model needs to estimate the positive quantized 201 representation v Qi in a set of quantized candidate representations V Q . Good representations are 202 learned by maximizing the agreement between output representation and quantized representation of 203 the same RoIs as follows: 204 where sim ( · , · ) computes the cosine similarity between two vectors, λ is a hyperparameter, and κ is a 205 temperature scalar. The second term encourages the model to use the codebook entries more equally. 206 Task #3 : Vision-Language Alignment. To enforce the alignment among different modalities, we 207 explicitly encourage alignment between words and image regions via similarity-preserving knowledge 208 distillation [30]. Note that, unlike the text-image alignment in LayoutLMv2 [6] which splits the 209 image into four regions and predicts whether the given word is covered or not on the image side, 210 we align the image and text belonging to the same region. The goal is to minimize the differences 211 between the pairwise similarities of sentence embeddings and the pairwise similarities of image 212 region features: 213 where S is the unmasked input sentence embeddings, H LV is the mapped visual representations of the 214 ﬁnal layer, || · || F is the Frobenius norm, and f Norm performs L2 normalization. 215 5

4 Experiment 216 4.1 Pretraining UniDoc 217

 Pretraining corpus. We build our pretraining corpus based on IIT-CDIP Test Collection 1.0 [31], 218 which contains more than 11M scanned document images. To differentiate pretraining from ﬁnetuning, 219 we ﬁlter out the document images of RVL-CDIP [10] from IIT-CDIP since it is a subset of IIT-CDIP, 220 and sample 1M document images as our pretraining corpus. 221 Table 1 shows the dataset statistics. IIT-CDIP 222 only provides the OCR texts in XML format. 223 We extract words and their locations by applying 224 EasyOCR [22] on document images. As shown 225 in Fig. 2 (a), EasyOCR provides two kinds of 226 output modes: non-paragraph and paragraph. 227 The paragraph mode groups the non-paragraph 228 results into text regions. We adopt the paragraph- 229 mode outputs as the basic input elements since 230 text regions provide semantically more meaning- 231 ful information than independent words. There 232 are some advantages to such a design: (1) the 233 region-level design hierarchically encodes document elements and this facilitates the modeling 234 of latent relationships at the region level which has higher-level semantics than the word level. 235 (2) the hierarchical encoding also overcomes the input size limitation of word-level BERT-based 236 models [5, 6]. 237 Pretraining setting. We initialize the sentence encoder f SentEnc with BERT-NLI-STSb-base [34] 238 pretrained for NLI [35] and STS-B [36]. The ResNet-50 backbone in the image encoder is pretrained 239 on the PubLayNet training set [33]. All the parameters (except f SentEnc and f ImEnc ) are randomly 240 initialized. During pretraining, we freeze the parameters of f SentEnc and jointly train the visual 241 encoder and multi-modal UniDoc model in an end-to-end fashion. Such an end-to-end training 242 allows the ConvNet and Transformer to realize their full potentials in spatial and sequence modeling 243 for pretraining. UniDoc contains 12 layers of gated cross-attention transformer blocks. We set the 244 hidden size to 768 and the number of heads to 12, the maximum number of regions N to 64, and the 245 maximum input sequence length for f SentEnc to 512. The pretraining is conducted on 8 NVIDIA Tesla 246 V100 32GB GPUs with a batch size of 64. It is trained with Adam optimizer [37], with an initial 247 learning rate of 10 − 5 , weight decay of 10 − 4 , and learning rate warmup in the ﬁrst 20% iterations. 248 To learn a useful multimodal representation, random masking is applied to both textual and visual 249 inputs. For MSM, we set the mask probability p s Mask for input sentences to 15%. 80% among the 250 masked sentences are replaced by special sentence [CLS, MASK, SEP], while 10% sentences are 251 replaced by random sentences sampled from other documents, and 10% remains unchanged. For 252 VCL, the λ is set to 0.1, κ is set to 0.1, the mask probability p v Mask is set to 7.5% and the masked RoI 253 features are ﬁlled with zeros. The temperature τ is annealed from 2.0 to 0.5 by a factor of of 0.999995 254 at every iteration. We select the pretraining checkpoint with the lowest L Pretraining for ﬁnetuning stage. 255

4.2 Finetuning Tasks

 256 Form Understanding. Form understanding requires the model to predict the label for each semantic 257 entity. We use FUNSD [32] as the evaluation dataset. It contains 149/50 training/testing images. 258 Fig 2 (b) shows a sample from FUNSD. Each semantic entity comprises a list of words, a label, and a 259 bounding box. The ofﬁcially-provided OCR texts and bounding boxes are used during training and 260 testing. We take the semantic entities as input and feed the concatenated visual and textual output 261 representations to a classiﬁer. We apply cross-entropy loss for ﬁnetuning. The model is ﬁnetuned 262 for 100 epochs with a learning rate of 10 − 5 and batch size of 16. All the parameters except f SentEnc 263 are trained. One of question , answer , header or other is predicted for each semantic entity. We use 264 entity-level F1 score as the evaluation metric. 265 Receipt Understanding. Receipt understanding requires the model to recognize a list of text lines 266 with bounding boxes. The performance on this task is evaluated on CORD [9] dataset. It contains 267 626/247 receipts for training/testing. The receipts are labeled with 30 types of entities under 4 268 6 categories: company , date , address , and total . Like FUNSD, we feed the concatenated visual and 269 textual output representations to the classiﬁer. The model is ﬁnetuned for 200 epochs with a batch 270 size of 16 and a learning rate of 10 − 5 . The evaluation metric is entity-level F1 score. 271 Document Classiﬁcation. Document classiﬁcation involves predicting the category for each doc- 272 ument image. We use RVL-CDIP [10] as the target dataset. It consists of 320K/40K/40K train- 273 ing/validation/testing images under 16 categories. The OCR words and bounding boxes are ex- 274 tracted by EacyOCR. To ﬁne-tune UniDoc on RVL-CDIP, we compute the overall representation 275 as an element-wise product between the visual and textual representations averaged from all sen- 276 tences/regions, and learn a classiﬁer on top of the overall representation with cross-entropy loss. We 277 ﬁne-tune the model for 30 epochs with a batch size of 64 and a learning rate of 10 − 5 . Classiﬁcation 278 accuracy over 16 categories is used to measure model performance. 279 Document Object Detection. Document object detection involves decomposing a document im- 280 age into semantic units. We evaluate the effectiveness of our pretrained visual backbone on Pub- 281 LayNet [33]. As shown Fig. 2 (d), the documents in PubLayNet are scientiﬁc articles. PubLayNet 282 consists of 336K/11K training/validation images with six category labels ( text , title , list , ﬁgure , and 283 table ). We train Faster-RCNN (F-RCNN) using Detectron2 [38] and initialize the visual backbone 284 with the pretrained ResNet-50 from UniDoc. The model is trained for 180k iterations with a base 285 learning rate of 0.01 and a batch size of 8. Mean average precision (MAP) @ intersection over union 286 (IOU) [0.50:0.95] of bounding boxes is used to measure the performance. 287

4.3 Results and Discussion

 288 The importance of multimodal learning. To study the effect of multimodal learning, we experiment 289 in three different settings (1) Vision only (V): this setting omits the textual components of UniDoc 290 and adopts multilayer self-attention transformer to learn the visual representation. (2) Language 291 only (L): this setting omits the visual encoder and keeps only the textual components. (3) Vision- 292 Language (V+L): this setting considers both vision and language information. We ﬁrst train three 293 settings without pretraining. Table 2 shows consistent improvement across tasks for V+L over the 294 single-stream baselines (V or L). This demonstrates that our UniDoc model is able to learn important 295 visual-linguistic relationships that beneﬁt downstream tasks even without pretraining. 296 In Table 2, we ﬁnd that visual information dominates the performance of document classiﬁcation, 297 while language information contributes a lot to form understanding and receipt understanding. The 298 results also indicate that different document tasks rely on different information. For document entity 299 recognition tasks, language information is more important than visual features. As can be seen in 300 Fig. 2 (b) and (c), entity recognition is more word-oriented. On the other hand, document classiﬁcation 301 is more focused on global-level understanding. As a result, visual and layout information contribute 302 a lot to the ﬁnal prediction of the document classiﬁcation model. This matches well with the innate 303 abilities of humans to distinguish between document types without fully understanding the words. 304 Effect of pretraining tasks. We analyze the effectiveness of different pretraining settings through ab- 305 lation studies over FUNSD, CORD, and RVL-CDIP, which are representative document benchmarks. 306 Table 2 ablates the key design choices in pretraining UniDoc. For experimental efﬁciency, UniDoc 307 7 models evaluated here are trained with 5 epochs on 300k training corpus. Overall, the pretraining of 308 UniDoc consistently improves the performance over all three downstream tasks. The improvement 309 gains vary among different tasks. 310 We ﬁrst establish two baselines: MSM+MVM in Table 2 indicates the combination of masked sen- 311 tence learning and masked visual feature prediction. Similar to MSM, for MVM, we freeze the visual 312 backbone and perform masked visual feature prediction via RoI-feature regression. MSM+VCL 313 jointly trains the visual backbone end-to-end with contrastive learning. As shown in the Table 2, 314 MSM+MVM achieves better results than the model without pretraining. Furthermore, when combin- 315 ing VCL together with MSM, consistent performance gains are observed across all the benchmarks. 316 Among the three ﬁnetuning tasks, the improvements on FUNSD and CORD are bigger than on 317 RVL-CDIP. We think the local context modeling capability of the ConvNet-based image encoder 318 brings more beneﬁts to entity recognition, since entities are heavily linked and correlated to their 319 local surroundings. When MSM, VCL, and VLA are jointly trained, we observe further performance 320 gains across all the benchmarks. We also consider the image-text matching task (Rel) [15, 6], and 321 combine Rel with MSM+VCL+VLA. It hurts the performance of all three downstream tasks. We 322 conjecture that the image-text matching task introduces mismatched pairs of image and OCR texts as 323 negative examples that potentially hamper the training of other tasks. 324 Performance Comparison with SoTA. We further pretrain UniDoc on 1M document images with 325 5 epochs and report the ﬁnetuning results in Table 3. UniDoc outperforms previous models on 326 FUNSD and CORD, by a signiﬁcantly large margin, demonstrating that our proposed approach is 327 highly effective, partially due to the end-to-end training of the image encoder that improves the 328 semantic alignments between images and texts. Note that UniDoc is pretrained on a subset of 329 IIT-CDIP (1M document images), which is considerably less than the 11M document images used in 330 LayoutLM [5] and LayoutLMv2 [6]. TILT [39] builds a 1.1M pretraining corpus by combining RVL- 331 CDIP, UCSF Industry Documents Library, and Common Crawl. UniDoc also achieves promising 332 results on document classiﬁcation. Note that both LayoutLM v2 and TILT use Microsoft OCR, which 333 is a commercial service with a stronger OCR performance than EasyOCR, which is used in our 334 experiments. We ﬁnd that OCR plays a key role in document classiﬁcation performance. As shown 335 in Fig. 3, UniDoc performs the best on the ‘ email ’ category but worst on the ‘ form ’ category. We also 336 compare the results of different OCR engines: EasyOCR [22] vs. Tesseract [40]. The results show 337 that UniDoc with EasyOCR achieves a better performance than with Tesseract. EasyOCR is powered 338 by an advanced neural network, while Tesseract is based on less sophisticated techniques. 339 8 Effect of visual backbone. Addition- 340 ally, we apply the trained visual back- 341 bone to document object detection 342 on PubLayNet. The performance of 343 the F-RCNN on the validation set is 344 depicted in Table 4. To better com- 345 pare, we establish two F-RCNN mod- 346 els with: (1) backbone initialized with 347 ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet; (2) backbone initialized from UniDoc’s pretrained visual back- 348 bone. It can be seen that our pretrained backbone outperforms ImageNet-pretrained backbones. 349 By leveraging UniDoc, we can train different variants of the visual backbone and apply them to 350 document-speciﬁc downstream applications, without relying on incompatible pretrained backbones 351 from other domains ( e.g ., natural image). Moreover, the visual backbone of UniDoc does not require 352 any custom layers, and thus any ConvNet architecture can be used in place of ResNet. 353

5 Conclusion, Limitations, and Future Works

 354 We develop UniDoc, a uniﬁed pretraining framework for document understanding. Our model 355 introduces a novel joint training framework that effectively exploits the visual and textual information 356 during pretraining and ﬁnetuning. We evaluate the UniDoc comprehensively on three downstream 357 tasks: form understanding, receipt understanding, and document image classiﬁcation. Extensive 358 empirical analysis demonstrates that the pretraining procedure can take advantage of multimodal 359 inputs and effectively aggregating and aligning visual and textual information of document images 360 with the proxy tasks. This work has a broader impact on document applications. By ﬁnetuning the 361 pretrained UniDoc on task-speciﬁc data, document processing systems can provide better results and 362 reduce the expensive data annotations costs. In terms of negative social impact, the document images 363 used for pretraining may contain sensitive information and therefore the models trained on such data 364 may inappropriately leak some private information. To address the privacy leakage, it is worthwhile 365 to explore the combination of privacy-preserving learning and self-supervised learning. 366 There are interesting short- and long-term research directions for UniDoc: (1) we freeze the sentence 367 encoder during pretraining and ﬁne-tuning phases due to computational constraints. A better doc- 368 ument representation can be learned by jointly training the sentence encoder, visual backbone and 369 cross-attention encoder in a completely end-to-end fashion. (2) Although impressive performance 370 has been achieved in document entity recognition tasks such as form and receipt understanding, the 371 classiﬁcation accuracy on semi-structured documents such as forms is still inferior to that of rich-text 372 documents. It is possible to devise a better method to model the spatial relationship among words. 373 (3) An interesting direction is to extend UniDoc to multipage/multilingual document pretraining. 374 Additionally, there exist many text-based labeled document datasets in the NLP domain, such as 375 document summarization. Can we transfer the knowledge learned from the text-based document 376 domain to the image-based document domain? Lastly, in addition to scanned documents, using digital 377 PDF as part of the pretraining data is a direction worth exploring since it provides rich metadata 378 which could be beneﬁcial for multimodal learning. 379 9

review.segment_text: Summary:
This paper proposes a self-supervised pre-training framework for document understanding called UniDoc.
UniDoc takes multimodal data (image feature and text feature from document) as input and uses gated cross attention for learning cross-modal correlation.
The superiority of UniDoc has been empirically shown on several downstream tasks.
Main Review:
This work proposes a pre-training method for document understanding by designing a model architecture and training objectives that can effectively utilize visual features as well as textual features.
UniDoc achieves remarkable performance improvement in downstream tasks such as entity recognition, document detection, and reception understanding.
Extensive experiments and ablation studies show that each component is beneficial.
While used components are effective, each of them is from other works.
In other words, UniDoc is a combination of well-known techniques, having a lack of significant novelty.
review.explanation: The reviewer mentions that there is a lack of novelty. However, combining all the techniques is non-trivial and creating an extensive framework can be novel as well.

review.segment_text: There is no performance comparison with word-level pre-training.
Since the entity recognition task is word-oriented as mentioned in the paper, it would be better to perform pre-training on word-level rather than sentence-level.
UniDoc does not improve the performance of document classification tasks over previous methods.
Although the authors argue that it attributes to the difference of OCR engines, they should explain the magnitude of this difference.
They say the difference between EasyOCR and Tesseract, but should report performance metric and qualitative analysis on the RVL-CDIP dataset to compare real Microsoft OCR and EasyOCR.
Limitations And Societal Impact:
I think interesting future research directions are described in the last section of the paper.

score.Code Of Conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

review.segment_text: Summary:
This paper proposes a new unified framework for pre-training image/text encoders for capturing both visual and textual features in documents.

The main Transformer model is pre-trained with multimodal embeddings as input features (visual & textual features) through three pre-training tasks: Masked Sentence Modeling, Visual Contrastive Learning, and Vision-Language Alignment.
The pre-trained model can be fine-tuned on downstream tasks on which the proposed UniDoc model outperforms baselines.
review.explanation: The proposed method doest not outperform all baselines on some tasks.

review.segment_text: Main Review:
[Originality]
I feel that the methods/model architectures are largely putting together existing pieces of work from vision and language fields.
In Sec 3.1, the proposed feature extraction, quantization, and gated cross-attention modules are all rather straightforward variations of existing work (i.e., the essential mechanisms are not new).
Although they do make sense and are reasonable choices for a multimodal framework, I see little novelty here.
review.explanation: In terms of application, combining exisitng techniques is non-trivial

review.segment_text: In Sec 3.2, the three pre-training tasks also resemble well-known techniques, like masked language modeling in NLP and contrastive learning in vision.
The alignment of vision and language is also not particularly surprising/insightful and provides little performance gain according to Table 2.
[Quality]
I do not find anything wrong with the proposed methods and models, since they are largely borrowed from well-known existing work.
However, the overall technical contribution does not seem exciting/insightful to me.
review.explanation: This claim is subjective and thus is an opinion more than a review.

review.segment_text: One thing that appears weird to me is the frozen sentence encoders initialized with the weights trained on NLI and STS datasets--if they have been fine-tuned on those tasks, they may capture too corpus-specific features to generalize to other domains (like the new pre-training documents).
I don't think the computational constraints are valid excuses if the paper is proposing a new pre-training framework, which should be generic and not task-specific.
[Clarity]
The paper is overall well-structured and the methods are explained clearly.
[Significance]
The effectiveness of the proposed model is evaluated on four downstream tasks, by comparing with a set of baselines.
The model outperforms the baselines with large margins.
review.explanation: The proposed method doest not outperform all baselines on some tasks.

review.segment_text: However, I am not sure how significant the results are to the NeurIPS community, because none of the baselines compared, as far as I am aware of, are from ML conferences.

I would like to see comparisons with stronger baselines (e.g., there are many better PLMs than BERT from the language field), and ideally how the framework performs on pure language applications (e.g.
the GLUE & SQuAD benchmarks) since a lot of applications may not have images and texts simultaneously.
If the UniDoc model cannot be effectively used for pure language applications, I am not convinced that this can be called a "unified" framework.
[Summary]
Pros:
The methods are clearly introduced with reasonable motivation.
The connection with existing work is described in detail.
The model has better empirical performance than a set of baselines on some specific applications.
Cons:
The methods are not novel/insightful as they are largely borrowing from existing work.
review.explanation: In terms of application, combining exisitng techniques is non-trivial

review.segment_text: There are concerns about the generalization ability of the model since it uses dataset-specific text encoders.

The significance of the empirical contribution made is not clear to me (the baselines are not from the ML community and are not strong enough; it's not clear whether the model works for pure language applications as a "unified" framework)
Limitations And Societal Impact:
I think the authors have made reasonable responses regarding the limitations and potential negative social impact of their work.
